coredata.dc:title: Luminescent solid-state carbon dots synthesized based on the one-step hydrothermal method for the immobilization of α-glucosidase and imaging screening of enzyme inhibitors
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Yi
Jiang, Xu
Wang, Xuchao
Gao, Xun
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: Modern pharmacology studies have demonstrated that α-glucosidase is closely associated with a variety of metabolic disorders. Natural herbs have emerged as possible sources of α-glucosidase inhibitors (AGIs) in recent years. Thus far, highly selective screening of biologically active chemicals in complicated mixtures using ligand fishing approaches has not been able to achieve biomedical imaging, which is critical for real-time identification. Hence, this paper presented a novel technique for screening potential AGIs in Scutellaria baicalensis by utilizing fluorescent carbon dots that have been immobilized with α-glucosidase (α-glu-NH2-CDs). The synthesized carbon dots materials exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, biocompatibility, and an abundance of functional groups, as demonstrated by the utilization of several techniques. The activity of α-glucosidase covalently linked to the material under ideal immobilization conditions was 86.81% of that of the free form. The immobilized α-glucosidase also exhibited great temperature stability, pH stability and reusability according to an examination of its enzymatic characteristics. As a result, three ligands were identified as potential AGIs, i.e., baicalein, wogonin and scullcapflavone II by UPLC-MS/MS. The molecular docking and enzyme inhibition test indicated that the isolated compounds exhibited significant α-glucosidase inhibitory activities. Moreover, the proliferation inhibitory activity of the screened ligands on HCT-116 cells was further verified morphologically using in situ imaging technique. These results strongly suggested that the established method, which combined ligand fishing with in situ visual imaging, not only screen and separate the potential AGIs in Scutellaria baicalensis quickly and effectively, but also provide an effective platform for screening bioactive compounds on different receptors.

head.ce:title: Luminescent solid-state carbon dots synthesized based on the one-step hydrothermal method for the immobilization of α-glucosidase and imaging screening of enzyme inhibitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modern pharmacology studies have demonstrated that α-glucosidase is closely associated with a variety of metabolic disorders. Natural herbs have emerged as possible sources of α-glucosidase inhibitors (AGIs) in recent years. Thus far, highly selective screening of biologically active chemicals in complicated mixtures using ligand fishing approaches has not been able to achieve biomedical imaging, which is critical for real-time identification. Hence, this paper presented a novel technique for screening potential AGIs in Scutellaria baicalensis by utilizing fluorescent carbon dots that have been immobilized with α-glucosidase (α-glu-NH2-CDs). The synthesized carbon dots materials exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, biocompatibility, and an abundance of functional groups, as demonstrated by the utilization of several techniques. The activity of α-glucosidase covalently linked to the material under ideal immobilization conditions was 86.81% of that of the free form. The immobilized α-glucosidase also exhibited great temperature stability, pH stability and reusability according to an examination of its enzymatic characteristics. As a result, three ligands were identified as potential AGIs, i.e., baicalein, wogonin and scullcapflavone II by UPLC-MS/MS. The molecular docking and enzyme inhibition test indicated that the isolated compounds exhibited significant α-glucosidase inhibitory activities. Moreover, the proliferation inhibitory activity of the screened ligands on HCT-116 cells was further verified morphologically using in situ imaging technique. These results strongly suggested that the established method, which combined ligand fishing with in situ visual imaging, not only screen and separate the potential AGIs in Scutellaria baicalensis quickly and effectively, but also provide an effective platform for screening bioactive compounds on different receptors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: α-Glucosidase located in the small intestinal mucosa, is extensively distributed in biological organisms and engaged in multiple biological processes such as digestion of food, biosynthesis of glycoproteins, and the synthesis and catabolism of polysaccharides and glycocalyxes [1]. It is an important enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of dietary polysaccharides into glucose, which can significantly affect postprandial blood glucose and insulin levels. And the digestion and absorption of dietary sugars are the main sources of blood sugar. Therefore, it is worthwhile to pay attention to the clinical application of α-glucosidase inhibitors (AGIs) in the treatment of diabetes mellitus (DM) [2,3]. Furthermore, in recent years, studies have indicated that AGIs inherently inhibit protein glycosylation and lipid glycosylation, which in turn possess anti-tumor [4,5], anti-viral [6,7] and other efficacy [8]. For this reason, the development of appropriate AGIs to regulate the activity of α-glucosidase for the treatment of a variety of diseases carries tremendous prospects for application.

Even though a series of effective AGIs have been developed, these drugs invariably cause serious adverse effects such as hypoglycemia and dysfunction of the liver and kidney [2,9]. Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), the Chinese heirloom treasure, exhibits a diverse chemical composition with few side effects [10]. As a result, an increasing number of reports on TCM are being developed as a valuable source of safe and effective AGIs [11,12]. Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi is a perennial herb of the Labiatae family, whose dried root has been widely used as a major herb for the treatment of DM in China for thousands of years. It contains diverse bioactive constituents, such as flavonoids, phenylpropanoids, steroids, volatile oil, and other chemical components [13,14]. Among them, the abundant flavonoids are the main chemical constituents, including baicalin, baicalein, wogonoside, wogonin, and so on. Modern pharmacological studies have demonstrated that they possess many therapeutic functions, such as anti-inflammatory, anti-tumor, antibacterial, hepatoprotective, neuroprotective, and others [15,16]. Furthermore, it was also shown that all flavonoids in Scutellaria baicalensis showed favorable α-glucosidase inhibitory effects [17,18]. For instance, baicalein can control the metabolism of glucose by interfering with the PI3K/Akt/GSK3β signaling pathway, NF-κB signaling pathway, and fatty acid synthesis, consequently lowering the production and release of inflammatory agents [19]. And previous studies have suggested that baicalein can regulate glucose metabolism in tumor cells by interfering glucose transport 1 (GLUT1) transport [20]. Wogonin additionally has the ability to decrease blood sugar through, among other things, reducing glucose uptake, sugar synthesis in the liver, and controlling the expression of rate-limiting enzymes during glucose metabolism [21].

Due to the complexity of chemical constituents in TCM, the extraction and screening of active constituents in natural products is a formidable challenge. The conventional screening procedure requires separation, purification, and identification. And numerous inactive compounds are also isolated along with active compounds during the procedure, which leads to the whole process being time-consuming, labor-intensive, and inefficient. Therefore, a rapid and highly efficient method for screening needs to be explored. With the accelerating advance of affinity separation techniques, ligand fishing technology based on protein target-ligand binding has been considered as an efficient technique to screen active components from complex TCM [22]. Only the compounds that exhibit attraction to the fixed target will be preserved for subsequent examination during the approach, whereas the unbound compounds will be eliminated from the mixture. Therefore, ligand fishing methods reveal the characteristics of specificity, high efficiency, simple operation, and high throughput. And in recent years, various ligand fishing techniques have been developed and successfully used for screening bioactive compounds from natural products, including affinity ultrafiltration [23], affinity chromatography [24], affinity capillary electrophoresis [25], enzyme immobilization[26,27] and cell membrane-coated electrochemical sensor [20]. Among them, enzyme immobilization coupled with LC-MS methods for screening enzyme inhibitors are the most effective techniques. Various types of solid carriers, such as metal-organic framework [28], magnetic nanoparticles [29], silica nanospheres [30] and others[31], have been successfully used to immobilize enzymes by chemical or physical methods and discover potential active molecules. But these solid carriers cannot accomplish biomedical imaging at the same time. As a result, Hu et al. created a novel technique that integrates solid-phase ligand fishing with an in situ imaging method to selectively enrich and detect inhibitors of heat shock protein 90 (Hsp 90) from the medicinal plants, Tripterygium wilfordii and Curcuma longa L.[32,33], firstly combining the screening and imaging of bioactive molecules based on fluorescent ligand fishing.

To date, numerous types of fluorescent probes have been deployed in cell imaging, including quantum dots [34], upconversion nanoparticles [35], and fluorescent metal nanoclusters [36]. However, their practical application in bioimaging of these fluorescent nanomaterials was limited because of high toxicity, low solubility in water, low fluorescence quantum yield, bad photo stability and others. Since 2004, Carbon Dots (CDS), a new carbon based zero-dimensional fluorescent material, has attracted enormous attention and developed a set of investigation [37]. Compared with traditional fluorescent probes, the CDS has numerous appealing merits, including superior optical characteristics, high solubility in water, lack of toxicity, eco-friendliness, versatility in raw material sources, strong biocompatibility, and more [38]. These excellent properties make CDS possess good application prospects in many fields, including medical imaging technology [39], environmental monitoring [40], chemical analysis [41], catalyst preparation [42] and energy development [43]. Moreover, in order to broaden its scope of applications, different modification methods could be adopted for imparting new functional groups on the surface of CDs, allowing them to be functionalized with enzymes or proteins through means such as physical blending or chemical bonding, including electrostatic forces, the NHS/EDC approach, and amidation [44].

Hence, we firstly synthesized amino-functionalized solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (NH2-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method from o-phenylenediamine and citric acid. Then, under the optimal enzyme immobilization and ligand fishing conditions, the α-glucosidase was successfully covalently attached to the NH2-CDs through cross linking of glutaraldehyde to screen AGIs from complex matrixes, Scutellaria baicalensis. The resulting ligands were identified by UPLC-MS/MS while the feasible binding mode between potential AGIs and enzyme were further verified by molecular docking. In addition, the validity of the method and the activity of the ligands were further verified and evaluated by in vitro enzyme inhibition assays and in situ cell imaging.

A one-step hydrothermal process has been employed to create NH2-CDs. In brief, citric acid (0.3844 g) and OPD (0.1729 g) were thoroughly dissolved in 18 mL of water, after which APTES (2 mL) was added and uniformly mixed into the above-mentioned solution. Finally, the mixture was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 5 h. The supernatant was removed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min, followed by three water washes and drying at 60 °C.

GA was selected as the cross-linking agent to immobilize α-glucosidase [45]. 20 mg of NH2-CDs were shaken with 1 mL of 10% GA solution in the constant temperature mixer at 800 rpm for 3 h at 4 °C and subsequently centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was discarded, and the precipitation was washed three times with PBS. The collected material was then mixed with α-glucosidase solution (1 mL, 2.0 U∙mL−1) and continued to oscillate at 4 °C for 4 h to achieve immobilization of the enzyme. After the reaction was over, the α-glu-NH2-CDs nanocomposites were obtained by centrifugation, washed three times with PBS, and then stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of immobilization and ligand fishing conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Luteolin, a typical α-glucosidase inhibitor [46], was utilized as the positive control for optimizing factors during the immobilization and ligand fishing procedures. The GA concentration (5%, 10%, 15%), enzyme concentration (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 U∙mL−1), cross-linking time (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 20, 24 h), incubation time (10, 20, 30, 45, 60 min), incubation temperature (4 ℃, 25 ℃, 37 ℃), incubation pH (pH 5, 6, 7, 8, 9), eluant concentration (100%, 90%, 70%, 50%, 10%), elution time (1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15 min) and eluant volume (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mL) were all optimized. And the efficiency of ligand fishing was estimated by comparing the recovery of luteolin. HPLC analysis was performed on a Diamonsil® C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm, 5 µm) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL∙min−1, column temperature of 30 ℃, injection volume of 10 µL, and detection wavelength of 350 nm. The mobile phases were 0.2% phosphoric acid-water (v/v) (A) and methanol (B) (45%: 55%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of enzymatic properties of immobilized α-glucosidase

ce:section.ce:para:
The quantity of α-glucosidase binding was calculated using the Bradford technique. In particular, 1 mL of various free α-glucosidase solution concentrations and 5 mL of Coomassie brilliant blue G-250 reagent (0.1 mg∙mL−1) were combined for 5 min before the absorbance at 595 nm was determined. The amount of α-glucosidase (mg∙mL−1) (x-axis) and the measured absorbance (y-axis) were used to create a calibration curve. The equation was used to determine the immobilization rate (Y%) of α-glucosidase.
(1) Y% = [(CF – CI) / CF] × 100
where CF and CI represent the quantities of free enzyme in the supernatant before and after immobilization, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The enzyme activity study for free and immobilized α-glucosidase
ce:section.ce:para: pNPG was employed as the substrate for the reaction and the absorbance of the product, pNP, was measured at 405 nm as part of a comparison of the enzyme activity of free and immobilized enzymes. Assumed to represent enzyme activity was the pNP peak area. 800 µL of PBS (20 mmol∙L−1, pH 7.0), 50 µL of either free α-glucosidase (2 U∙mL−1) or immobilized α-glucosidase (25 mg∙mL−1) and 150 µL of pNPG (20 mmol∙L−1) were sequentially added to a 5.0 mL Eppendorf tube. The enzymatic reaction was stopped by adding 2 mL of Na2CO3 (1 mol∙L−1) after the reaction mixture had been incubated at 60 °C for 10 min. UV–vis spectroscopy was used to determine the absorbance of mixture at 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and pH on the stability of immobilized α-glucosidase
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction mixtures were incubated at various temperatures—20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, and 70 °C—and the activities were compared in accordance with the described experimental procedure—in order to evaluate the thermal stability of free and immobilized enzymes. pH is also a crucial factor for enzyme activity. The pH stability of the free and immobilized enzymes was tested in the pH range of 5.0 to 9.0 using the aforementioned experimental technique. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

Under ideal temperature and pH conditions, the initial enzyme activity was assessed using the enzyme activity test method mentioned above. The immobilized enzyme was then centrifuged to separate it, cleaned with PBS to get rid of any leftover material, and added to a new reaction solution for the next experiment. To assess if the immobilized enzyme could be reused, the procedure was carried out five times. 100% initial enzyme activity was used as the standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme kinetics study of free and immobilized α-glucosidase
ce:section.ce:para:
By using the Lineweaver-Burk equation, the Michaelis constant (Km) values of free and immobilized α-glucosidase were calculated. With the enzyme concentration constant, the kinetics of the enzymatic reactions of free and immobilized α-glucosidase were examined at pNPG concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 4.0 mmol∙L−1. By comparing 1/V and 1/[S], Lineweaver-Burk plots, also double inverse curves, were created. The intercepts were used to compute the Km values for free and immobilized α-glucosidase. Every experiment was run in triplicate in parallel.
(2) 1/V = (Km/Vmax) × (1/[S]) + (1/Vmax)
where V and Vmax stand for the initial and maximum rates of the enzyme reaction, respectively, and [S] is the concentration of substrate.

A model mixture containing salicylic acid and ibuprofen (unexpected AGIs), catechin, apigenin, chlorogenic acid and luteolin (known AGIs) was established to test the veracity and specificity of the method. Briefly, 1.0 mL of the model mixture was incubated with 20 mg α-glu-NH2-CDs at 4 ℃ for 20 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant (S0) was obtained, and then the precipitate was washed with 3 × 1 mL PBS (S1-S3). Finally, the active ligands fished by the α-glu-NH2-CDs were eluted by 3 × 1 mL of 100% methanol (S4-S6). Subsequently, the model mixture and S0-S6 were chromatographically analyzed on the Diamonsil® C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm, 5 µm). The mobile phase consisted of solvent A (0.2% phosphoric acid-water) and solvent B (methanol); the elution conditions were as follows: 0–4 min (25–35% B), 4–13 min (35–85% B), 13–20 min (85–90% B), 20–21 min (90–25% B), 21–25 min (25% B); the detection wavelength was set at 230 nm.

Scutellaria baicalensis powders (20 g) were extracted twice by the refluxing method with 200 mL of 70% ethanol-water (v/v) for 1.5 h. Followed by filtration, and the combined filtrate was concentrated and dried using a rotary evaporator. Finally, the product was dissolved with methanol into a storage solution (100 mg∙mL−1) and then stored at 4 ℃ for further experiments.

Firstly, 1.0 mL of the extraction solution (25 mg∙mL−1) was incubated for 20 min at 4 ℃ with 20 mg of α-glu-NH2-CDs. Following centrifugation, the precipitate was washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4) to remove compounds with poor or no affinity. Finally, the ligands fished by the α-glu-NH2-CDs were eluted by 100% methanol for 10 min to generate eluent. Subsequently, the eluent was analyzed by UPLC-MS/MS.

The samples were performed on an ACQUITY™ UPLC™ system (Waters, Milford, MA, USA) in conjunction with a triple-quadrupole tandem mass spectrometer equipped with an electrospray ionization (ESI) source. The chromatographic separation was decided to be carried out on a BEH C18 column (2.1 × 100 mm, 1.7 µm; Waters, Milford, MA, USA) with a 10 µL injection volume at 35 ℃. The mobile phase was composed of solvent A (10 mmol∙L−1 ammonium acetate water) and solvent B (methanol) at a flow rate of 0.2 mL∙min−1. The gradient elution conditions were as follows: 0–30 min (10–90% B), 30–31 min (90–10% B), 31–35 min (10% B). The detection of all samples was performed in full scan (MS Scan) and negative electrospray ionization (ESI-) mode. The optimal parameters were set as follows: capillary voltage 2.5 kV; source temperature 110 ℃; desolvation temperature 450 ℃. The desolvation gas was nitrogen with a flow rate of 500 L∙h−1.

To further forecast the powerful activity of ligands, molecular docking can fully and practically examine the interaction between the enzyme and ligands. The crystal structure of α-glucosidase (PDB ID: 3A4A; resolution: 1.6 Å) was downloaded from Protein Data Bank (https://www.rcsb.org/) for docking assay[47,48]. PyMOL software was implemented to eliminate the α-glucosidase's non-protein structures, including its original ligands (GLC), metal ions, and water molecules. Autodock Tools (ver. 1.5.6) was used to process the enzyme and the 3D structures of the ligands, and the resulting files were stored as receptor.pdbqt and ligand.pdbqt, respectively. After semi-flexible docking, the conformation with the lowest binding energy was selected as the best docking model. Ultimately, PyMOL software was applied to examine the binding interactions between the ligand and α-glucosidase.

50 µL of α-glycosidase solution (2.0 U∙mL−1) and 20 µL of test solution (the extract, eluent and control) were fully premixed and incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min. Then, 150 µL of pNPG (10 mmol∙L−1) as substrate and 800 µL PBS (0.1 mol∙L−1, pH 6.8) were added to the mixture to continue the reaction. After incubation at 37 ℃ for another 30 min, the enzymatic reaction was terminated by adding 2 mL of Na2CO3 (1 mol∙L−1). The absorbance of the released p-NP was measured at 405 nm using a UV spectrometer. Three parallel operations were done. And the same ligand fishing with inactive α-glu-NH2-CDs (obtained by heating at 80 ℃ for 1 h) was carried out as the negative control. The α-glucosidase inhibition ratio (%) was calculated according to the following Eq. (3):
(3) Inhibition ratio (%) = [1-(AS-ASB)/(AC-ACB)] × 100%
where AS is the absorption measured in the presence of different types of test solution; AC is the absorption measured without test solution; ASB and ACB are respectively the absorption of sample blank and control blank.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity study of nanoparticles by CCK-8 assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The CCK-8 assay was utilized to evaluate the cytotoxicity of NH2-CDs[49]. In detail, HCT-116 cells were inoculated at 5 × 103 cells∙well−1 in the 96-well plate containing DMEM complete medium at 37 ℃ in 5% CO2 for 24 h to achieve adherence. The medium was subsequently replaced with those containing different concentrations of NH2-CDs at 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 µg∙mL−1, and the cells were incubated for 12 h, 36 h and 48 h, while cells that had not been treated with nanoparticles served as the negative control. After that, the cells were washed with PBS, and 10 µL of CCK-8 solution (5 mg∙mL−1) was incorporated into each well to continue the incubation for 4 h. Ultimately, the absorbance was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader to estimate the cell viability. The experiment was carried out in triplicate. The cell viability (%) was determined using the subsequent Eq. (4):
(4) Cell viability (%) = (ASample-ABlank)/(AControl-ABlank) × 100%
where ASample, AControl and ABlank represent the absorbance of experimental wells, control wells and blank wells, respectively.

HCT-116 cells were inoculated at a density of 1.25 × 105 cells∙well−1 in the sterile 6-well plate and incubated in an incubator at 37 °C under 5% CO2 atmosphere for 12 h to adhere to the wall. The 25 µg∙mL−1 stocking solution of nanoparticles was generated by dispersing the α-glu-NH2-CDs loaded with ligands into complete DMEM. Then the original medium was discarded, and 200 µL stocking solution of nanoparticles was inserted to continue incubation for 12 h. After washing three times with PBS, the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min. Following that, 1 mL of PBS containing 0.3% triton as well as 2.5 µL PI dye was inserted to stain the fixed cells avoiding light for 30 min, and the fluorescence of the treated cells was observed by CLSM. The same operation was performed while using simple α-glu-NH2-CDs as the control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the NH2-CDs and α-glu-NH2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the NH2-CDs were observed by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the SEM image demonstrated that the synthesized NH2-CDs were spherical. Meanwhile, the prepared spheres exhibited relatively uniform size with irregular aggregation. Fig. 1b displayed the TEM image of NH2-CDs. The synthesized nanoparticles possessed smooth outer surface and displayed darker regions towards the center of the nanospheres, indicating that the carbon core of the synthesized nanoparticles are encapsulated by the “Si-O-Si” network structure [50]. As shown in Fig. 1c, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum excited that a strong emission of NH2-CDs was around 570 nm at 469 nm excitation wavelength. It is demonstrated that the nanocomposites had good optical properties and bright-yellow emission, which can not only permeate strongly into tissues but also effectively avoid the autofluorescence interference of biological samples [51]. Thus, it would be able to act as biocompatible material for in situ cell imaging.

Simultaneously, Fig. 1c also showed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of α-glucosidase, NH2-CDs and α-glu-NH2-CDs. The α-glucosidase exhibited strong absorption at 208 nm and 255 nm. Compared with the NH2-CDs, α-glu-NH2-CDs also displayed apparent UV absorbance as α-glucosidase, further confirming the successful immobilization of this enzyme on the NH2-CDs.

The surface elemental compositions of the NH2-CDs were further confirmed by the XPS. As shown in Fig. 2a, the full XPS spectrum displayed four major peaks at 103.10, 285.04, 400.91 and 530.98 eV, attributing to Si 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s respectively. To be specific, the XPS C1s spectrum in Fig. 2b was mainly deconvoluted into five peaks of 287.44, 285.95, 285.19, 284.56 and 283.96 eV, corresponding to CO, C−O, C−N, C−C and C−Si, respectively [8,52]. Fig. 2c showed that the N 1 s spectrum exhibited two main peaks at 399.61 and 400.67 eV, indicating the existence of N − C and N − H [49,53]. In the O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d), there are three oxygen types at the positions of 532.21, 531.54 and 530.81 eV, representing Si−O, C−O and CO, respectively [8]. Meanwhile, the Fig. 2e displayed two characteristic Si 2p electronic states at 101.54 eV (Si−C) and 102.20 eV (Si−O) [45,53].

As shown in Fig. 2f, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to analyze the functional groups of α-glucosidase, NH2-CDs and α-glu-NH2-CDs, to further determine whether the bond of N-terminus of α-glucosidase onto NH2-CDs was achieved or not. The strong and broad absorption bands at 3610–3135 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching vibration of −OH and −NH2 on the surface of NH2-CDs. The typical signals at 1657 cm−1, 1563 cm−1, 755 cm−1, 697 cm−1 were arisen from the stretching vibration of −CO and the bending vibration of −N − H [54,55]. And the peaks at 1134 cm−1, 1036 cm−1 owing to Si−O−Si and Si−O−C bonds represented the APTES being linked to the surface of carbon cores and further forming the cross-linked “Si−O−Si” network structure by hydrolyzation. Overall, these results were consistent with the XPS data, confirming that the NH2-CDs were successfully synthesized[53]. Moreover, compared with the spectra of NH2-CDs, the new sharp adsorption peak at 1415 cm−1 appeared to correspond to the CN bond, and the peaks at 2940 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C−CH2 −C. It is suggested that the Schiff base was formed between the NH2-CDs and α-glucosidase by cross-linking reaction [33,45,56], which will possess a good stability [57].

As the immobilization conditions had a great impact on the enzyme loading capacity, while ligand fishing conditions would influence the quantity of ligands bound to α-glucosidase [27,32], the processes of immobilization, incubation and elution using luteolin investigated the parameters including enzyme concentration, concentration of cross-linking agents, immobilization time, incubation temperature, incubation time, incubation pH, elution time and eluent. Fig. 3a illustrated that the eluent's relative recovery experienced a positive trend until it reached its peak value at 10% GA concentration, after which it declined. The reason for this could be that excessive GA could also cross-link the enzyme protein, destroying the secondary structure of the enzyme protein and denaturing them [27]. As seen in Fig. 3b, the impact of α-glucosidase concentration (0.5–4.0 U∙mL−1) was also examined. The highest degree of relative activity was seen when the enzyme concentration was increased to 2.0 U∙mL−1. Nevertheless, as the amount of enzyme increased further, the relative activity steadily declined. This might occur as additional enzymes cause aggregation and affect the substrate’s binding to the active site [48]. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, the effect of cross-linking time (1–24 h) was investigated, suggesting that the activity of α-glu-NH2-CDs showed a gradual upward trend and tended to be saturated, and reached its maximum level when the covalent immobilization time was 4 h. Under ideal immobilization circumstances, the relevant parameters of the ligand fishing process were optimized (Fig. 3d-i). Ultimately, the optimal conditions for ligand fishing were as follows: the enzyme-immobilized material was incubated with the objective solution (pH 7.0) at 4 ℃ for 20 min and 1 mL of 100% methanol was chosen as the eluent to separate the bound components by eluting for 10 min

Although the characterization results demonstrated that the α-glu-NH2-CDs had been manufactured effectively, further research is necessary to identify their characteristics because this will be crucial for the upcoming affinity screening [58]. The standard curve of the protein content of α-glucosidase plotted by the Bradford method was used to determine the amount of α-glucosidase in the supernatant before and after immobilization. According to the computed data, the immobilization rate was 81.63% and the α-glucosidase content of α-glu-NH2-CDs was 0.08 U∙mg−1. The enzymatic activity of immobilized enzyme serves as the determining factor for the enzymatic reaction in enzyme immobilization investigations. As a result, based on the immobilization rate, the same concentration of free enzyme was generated. The relative activities of α-glu-NH2-CDs were then assessed by contrasting the absorbance of each sample catalyzed by free and immobilized enzyme. The immobilized α-glucosidase was successful and extremely active, as seen by the results, which revealed that its activity was 86.81% of that of free α-glucosidase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature and pH on enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve high enzyme activity, reaction pH and temperature were examined since they have a significant impact on the enzymatic reaction [59]. The reaction pH was fixed at 7.0, and the relative activity were measured and compared at various temperatures in the range of 20–70 °C in order to evaluate the thermal stability of immobilized and free enzyme. The effect of temperature on free and immobilized α-glucosidase followed a similar pattern, as shown in Fig. 4a, with enzyme activity rising as temperature rose from 20 °C to 60 °C. Both displayed their peak enzyme catalytic activity at 60 °C. The fact that excessive temperatures might change the spatial structure of the enzyme, denaturing the enzyme molecule and causing a loss of enzyme activity, may be the cause of the drop in enzyme activity at temperatures greater than 60 °C [59]. Further evidence that immobilization considerably improved the heat resistance of α-glucosidase was found in the fact that the immobilized enzyme was more active than the free enzyme at all temperatures tested. This is explained by the fact that the covalent bond that forms between the enzyme and the solid phase carrier after the enzyme is immobilized reduces conformational flexibility and safeguards it from distortion or damage by heat exchange, making it more rigid to preserve the active structure [27].

At ideal temperature, Fig. 4b illustrated the pH-dependent variation in enzyme activity of free and immobilized α-glucosidase. When the pH was raised from 5.0 to 7.0, the relative activity of the immobilized enzyme increased and, like the free enzyme, reached a maximum at pH 7.0. The immobilized enzyme demonstrated better enzyme activity and pH stability than the free enzyme, although the catalytic activity of both enzymes gradually declined when the pH was in the range of 7.0 to 9.0. These findings suggested that the immobilized enzyme exhibited improved acid and basic resistance, which is probably because immobilization stabilizes the structure and conformation of the enzyme molecule. Since the majority of the pNPG were hydrolyzed at higher pH, the similarities between the activity of the immobilized and free enzymes at pH 9.0 can be explained [60].

By employing the same samples again and again in catalytic processes, the reuseability of α-glu-NH2-CDs was assessed [28]. As can be shown in Fig. 4c, it is depicted that after three cycles of use, α-glu-NH2-CDs still had 73.3% of its starting activity. The deactivation of the enzyme owing to deformation or shedding of the enzyme during washing after continuous usage can be the cause of the decline in enzyme activity with increasing number of uses. The fact that 40.9% of the relative activity persisted after five cycles after immobilization supported the ability of enzyme to be recycled. According to the findings, immobilized α-glucosidase will be significantly more reusable than free enzyme for a single usage, which can save operating costs and boost economic efficiency in real-world applications [61].

The most significant constant for researching the kinetics of enzymatic reactions is Km, which might represent the strength of the enzyme's affinity for the substrate. The affinity is weaker the higher the value of Km, and vice versa [62]. Plotting 1/V against 1/[S] in accordance with Eq. (2) allowed for the construction of the double inverse curves for both free and immobilized enzymes (Fig. 4d). The fitting process showed that the linear regression equations for the free and immobilized enzymes were, respectively, 1/V = (1.3456 ± 0.0457) × (1/[S]) + (1.1442 ± 0.0966) (R2 = 0.9942) and 1/V = (3.3587 ± 0.1208) × (1/[S]) + (1.3303 ± 0.0558) (R2 = 0.9936). As determined by the slope and intercept, the immobilized α-glucosidase (1.17 mmol∙L−1) exhibited a lower Km value than the free enzyme (2.52 mmol∙L−1), suggesting that the α-glu-NH2-CDs were more biophilic for pNPG. Under ideal enzymatic reaction circumstances, there is a higher likelihood of substrate contact with the active site of enzyme and a lesser diffusion barrier effect, which could be the cause of this [63]. The Km values of the immobilized enzymes also did not match those previously reported literatures [63,64], which could have something to do with the immobilization techniques, carrier materials, enzymatic reaction conditions, or the source of the enzymes.

To ascertain whether ligand fishing screening is viable and discriminatory, the α-glu-NH2-CDs were incubated with a standard mixture model that included salicylic acid, ibuprofen (as negative control), catechin, apigenin, chlorogenic acid and luteolin (as positive control). The HPLC chromatograms of the model mixture and S0-S6 are shown in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. 5S4–6, catechin, apigenin, chlorogenic acid and luteolin were effectively screened out from the mixture, but also the peak area of them decreased sequentially as the elution number increased. In addition, salicylic acid and ibuprofen were completely washed off by PBS since they do not bind to the α-glu-NH2-CDs. The results demonstrated that the proposed method was capable of selectively screening AGIs from the mixture model and might be utilized to effectively screen active small molecules from herbal extracts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UPLC-MS/MS analysis of compounds screened from Scutellaria baicalensis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 illustrated the MS Scan chromatograms of Scutellaria baicalensis extract (a), supernatant after ligand fishing (b) and eluate after ligand fishing (c). A total of three compounds were inferred in the eluate after ligand fishing compared with the herbal extract, and the detailed UPLC-MS/MS data were listed in Table S1. According to the mass spectrometry data, peak 1 (t R = 13.511 min) manufactured [M - H]- ions at m/z 269.64. The [M - H]- ion at m/z 269.64 created three fragment ions at 250.91 [M - H-H2O]-, 241.44 [M - H -CO]-, 223.30 [M - H -H2O-CO]- and 195.26 [M - H -CO-H2O-CO]-. Peak 1 was recognized as baicalein considering this information agreed with the mass spectral data of the control (Fig. S1a,c). The deprotonated molecular ions [M - H]- was observed at m/z 283.69 in MS Scan of peak 2 (t R = 15.169 min). The former yielded two major fragment ions at m/z 268.28 and m/z 239.33. The ion at m/z 268.28 resulted from the loss of −CH3 and the ion at m/z 239.33 occurred due to the loss of −CH3 and −COH. It was ultimately identified as wogonin using data from the control's mass spectrometry (Fig. S1b,d). Peak 3 at t R 21.350 min yielded an [M - H]- ion at m/z 373.72. Its Daughter Scan spectrum gave ions at m/z 343.32 [M - H-2CH3]- and m/z 300.28 [M – H-3CH3-CO]-. Compared to the earlier study results, the compound was validated as scullcapflavone II [17].

To better interpret the binding mechanisms and modes between the three ligands identified by UPLC-MS/MS and α-glucosidase, we investigated luteolin (the positive control) and all the selected ligands by molecular docking and calculated the binding energy of them, the results of which were listed in Table S2. Binding energy below 0 indicate that the two molecules are capable of spontaneous binding, with a higher negative value suggesting a more stable conformation. According to Table S2, the binding energies of baicalein and wogonin (−8.6 and −8.1 kcal/mol, respectively) were lower than those of luteolin, the positive control (−7.9 kcal/mol), indicating that these two substances have a stronger affinity for the enzyme. Furthermore, baicalein, wogonin, and scullcapflavone II all easily entered the active pocket of a-glucosidase and interacted with the key amino acid residues, as shown in Fig. 7. Among them, baicalein showed the lowest binding energy and possessed stronger enzyme inhibitory activity; its 3D docking pattern with α-glucosidase was presented in Fig. 7a. The hydroxy group of baicalein formed two hydrogen bonds with GLU411 and ARG315 of α-glucosidase. There are also hydrophobic interactions through the benzene ring with TYR72, PHE178 and PHE159 of the enzyme, as well as a π-cation interaction with ARG442. In addition, the amino acid residues of ASP69, ASP215, ASP352 and PHE303 were bound to baicalein via anion-π interaction. As shown in Fig. 7b, wogonin situated the active site through hydrogen bonding with ASP352 and GLU277. Hydrophobic, π-π, and alkyl-π interactions from PHE159, PHE1178 and PHE303 respectively, were also present. The interaction of scullcapflavone II with α-glucosidase was as shown in Fig. 7c. Four hydrogen bonds were formed through scullcapflavone II's interactions with the amino acid residues ASP69, GLU411, TYR158, and ARG442 as well as a hydrophobic force with TYR72. The molecular docking results demonstrated that all three compounds identified by ligand fishing were capable of binding to α-glucosidase, further validating the enzyme inhibitory activity of ligands.

The inhibitory activity of the herbal extract and the eluent incubated with α-glu-NH2-CDs on α-glucosidase was investigated using the eluent incubated with inactive α-glu-NH2-CDs as the negative control, and the results were shown in Fig. 8a. Enzyme inhibition of the extract was 47.6 ± 2.04% at the same concentration, while enzyme inhibition of the target component, or the eluate following ligand drape, was 70.67 ± 2.43%. The target component exhibited more inhibition than the crude sample, which might perhaps be attributed to the crude sample's lower concentration of active chemicals [58]. Moreover, the inhibition rate of the control group was only 10.3 ± 1.67%, which further proved the effectiveness and feasibility of the method.

Aiming to investigate the biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of NH2-CDs and to decrease the interference of nanomaterials themselves on the activity of HCT-116 cells, the CCK-8 assay was adopted to determine the effect of different concentrations of nanomaterials on cell viability at different dosing times so that to explore the most suitable dosing conditions and lay the foundation for in situ imaging screening. The results of the analysis were presented in Fig. 8b, which showed that with increasing nanomaterial concentrations (12.5–200 µg∙mL−1), the viability of HCT-116 cells remained above 80% after incubating for 12 h. Furthermore, at concentrations up to 200 µg∙mL−1, the cell viability was over 60% even if incubated for 48 h. It demonstrated that NH2-CDs possess low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which could avoid interference with the cellular condition due to its own causes for in situ imaging.

The pathogenesis associated with DM and the pathophysiologic mechanisms of its complications have been intimately linked to the development of a variety of diseases. According to studies, people with DM are more likely to develop colorectal cancer [65]. The ability of AGIs to outcompete glucose transporters for binding sites found in the membranes of colon cancer cells prevents the cancer cells from absorbing glucose, which in turn slows the growth of the cancer cells. Therefore, the hunt for medications with cytotoxic activity against colon cancer cell lines and attenuation of postprandial hyperglycemia may present a chance to create brand-new treatments for diabetic people [66]. In addition, since several bioactive components in Scutellaria baicalensis are considered to inhibit the growth of tumor cells by regulating glucose metabolism [20], thus effective screening of potential AGIs in it is of great significance. Due to these factors, we co-incubated α-glu-NH2-CDs loaded with ligands and pure α-glu-NH2-CDs with HCT-116 cells for cellular imaging in order to confirm the pharmacological activity of possible AGIs. The outcomes were displayed in Fig. 9. It was observed from the figure that when the cells were incubated with 25 µg∙mL−1 of nanoparticle solution for 4 h, the distinct yellow fluorescence was observed around the nucleus, which indicated that the material could enter the cells by encapsulation. Notably, treatment with ligand-loaded α-glu-NH2-CDs enabled to inhibit the proliferation of HCT-116 cells compared to pure α-glu-NH2-CDs, and the cells shrunk and died by apoptosis with nuclear membrane rupture. The results revealed that the AGIs fished restrain the growth and induce apoptosis of colon cancer cells, further confirming the effectiveness of the method.

In this study, the highly fluorescent CDs with abundant amino functional groups and low cytotoxicity were prepared by the simple one-step synthesis approach. Also, α-glucosidase was successfully immobilized on the surface of the nanomaterial using glutaraldehyde as the cross-linking agent. Based on the enzyme-immobilized fluorescent material as the solid-phase carrier, a ligand screening method combined with in situ imaging was established for screening potential AGIs from Scutellaria baicalensis. Under optimal conditions, a total of three components were screened from the herbal extract and identified by UPLC-MS/MS as baicalein, wogonin and scullcapflavone II, respectively. According to the molecular docking studies, these three ligands mostly engaged in hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions to access the active region of α-glucosidase. They exhibited significant enzyme inhibitory actions as compared to the positive control. Furthermore, in vitro enzyme inhibition assays and real-time cell imaging were also used to confirm the biological activities of the selected ligands. In conclusion, this method can be applied for the rapidly and efficiently screening and identification of bioactive compounds against multiple target proteins from natural products with complex compositions, providing a powerful tool for novel drug development.
